Over the past few years, I've tried almost everything. I started writing on Medium, but then overindexed on what was successful there and realized it changed what I was writing, because I wanted to be successful.Then I went to Substack. Then Ghost. Then back to Substack. Tried a bunch of random ones too that have come and gone (don't even remember what they were called haha)In terms of social media, Twitter is the one I can't get away from, but also tried out Threads, Mastodon, Nostr, BlueSky, Warpcast, and some others. And still do a little bit on LinkedIn, although trying to make myself do more there.Any more, it's mostly just Twitter, LinkedIn, one of my Ghost blogs, and I've started using Substack again to just share multipart essays. That way, on Ghost I can write all sorts of things but not make my newsletter have to bounce around with me as much.

Have you seen the work @GillVerd is doing with @Extropic_AI? I've been thinking about how to write algorithms for that kind of architecture (while having very little knowledge of the specifics of it)The way I look at it, information gradients are basically the "depth" of knowledge. In other words, what's the abstraction after which things stop being coherent and devolve into probabilities instead of predictions.

People would hate billionaires less if their time wasn’t being mined for data so that others can get rich. 

Conscious economics solves this problem and should be the goal of the billionaires.

I discovered a new failure mode of mine today.

I put out a lot of things, and I don't get much feedback. So when I do get feedback on something, I tend to try to rapidly iterate and get more feedback from anyone who dared give me some.

This means I tend to overload people.

The world moves on trust. 

It’s hard to see right now, but it does.
